events.title: Treaty of Paris — European Coal and Steel Community Founded
events.detail: France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg signed the Treaty of Paris, establishing the European Coal and Steel Community (ECSC). The treaty placed control of the key war-making industries under a supranational High Authority, making future Franco-German war structurally impossible. Jean Monnet became the first President of the High Authority.

events.title: Treaty of Rome — EEC and Euratom Founded
events.detail: The six ECSC members signed two treaties in Rome: the Treaty establishing the European Economic Community (EEC) and the Treaty establishing the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom). The EEC aimed to create a common market and customs union. This moment is widely regarded as the foundational act of what would become the European Union.

events.title: EEC Customs Union Completed Ahead of Schedule
events.detail: The EEC completed its internal customs union 18 months ahead of the Treaty of Rome's schedule. All tariffs on trade between member states were eliminated and a common external tariff (CET) was introduced. This was the first major achievement of the common market project and a significant boost to intra-Community trade.

events.title: De Gaulle Vetoes UK Membership for the First Time
events.detail: French President Charles de Gaulle delivered a press conference in Paris vetoing Britain's first application to join the EEC, which had been submitted in 1961. De Gaulle cited Britain's 'deep-seated hostility' to the European project and its 'special relationship' with the United States as incompatible with Community membership. The veto shocked other member states and delayed enlargement by a decade.

sources.name: BBC — UK and Europe: a troubled relationship

events.title: Luxembourg Compromise — National Veto Preserved
events.detail: After France's 'empty chair policy' paralyzed Council meetings for seven months, the Luxembourg Compromise was reached. It established that when a member state invokes 'very important national interests,' the Council must reach unanimous agreement rather than use qualified majority voting. This effectively gave each state a veto and slowed Community decision-making for two decades.

events.title: First Enlargement: UK, Ireland, and Denmark Join
events.detail: The United Kingdom, Ireland, and Denmark became members of the European Communities, expanding the original six to nine member states. After de Gaulle's resignation in 1969, negotiations proceeded under his successor Georges Pompidou. Norway had negotiated membership but rejected it in a referendum in September 1972. This enlargement effectively doubled the Community's English-speaking population.

sources.name: UK National Archives — Treaty of Accession 1972

events.title: Greece Joins — Mediterranean Enlargement Begins
events.detail: Greece became the 10th member of the European Communities, the first Mediterranean country to join. Greece had applied in 1975 after the restoration of democracy following the military junta. The accession was partly motivated by a desire to consolidate Greece's democratic transition. It opened the door to the 'Mediterranean enlargement' that would later include Spain and Portugal.

events.title: Spain and Portugal Join the Community
events.detail: Spain and Portugal acceded to the European Communities as the 11th and 12th members, completing the Mediterranean enlargement. Both countries had emerged from long authoritarian regimes — Spain under Franco and Portugal under Salazar/Caetano — and accession was seen as a democratic anchor. Their entry also significantly expanded the EEC's southern agricultural sector and prompted complex negotiations over fishing rights and structural funds.

events.title: First Direct Elections to the European Parliament
events.detail: For the first time, citizens of the nine EEC member states voted directly for Members of the European Parliament (MEPs). Previously, MEPs had been appointed by national parliaments. Simone Veil, a French politician and Holocaust survivor, became the first directly elected President of the European Parliament. Turnout averaged around 63%, though this would decline in subsequent elections.

events.title: Schengen Agreement Signed — Border-Free Travel Begins
events.detail: Five of the ten EEC members (France, West Germany, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg) signed the Schengen Agreement aboard a riverboat on the Moselle River near the Luxembourg village of Schengen. The accord abolished systematic border controls between signatory states. The convention implementing Schengen was signed in 1990 and entered into force in 1995, eventually expanding to encompass most EU and some non-EU European states.

events.title: Single European Act — First Major Treaty Revision
events.detail: The Single European Act (SEA) was the first substantial revision of the EEC Treaty. It set a deadline of December 31, 1992 for the completion of the internal single market, introduced qualified majority voting for most single-market legislation (removing the national veto for these areas), formally recognized the European Council, and strengthened the European Parliament's role. It was the legislative engine behind the '1992 project.'

events.title: Berlin Wall Falls — Reunification and Enlargement Horizon Shift
events.detail: The fall of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989 transformed the European integration project's geopolitical context. German reunification was achieved on October 3, 1990, immediately expanding the EEC territory eastward (the former East Germany joined as part of unified Germany). The collapse of communist regimes across Central and Eastern Europe opened the prospect of a major future enlargement to the east, fundamentally reorienting EU strategic thinking.

sources.name: European Commission — German Reunification and the EC

events.title: European Single Market Formally Completed
events.detail: The European Single Market came into full legal force on January 1, 1993, enabling free movement of goods, services, capital, and people (the 'four freedoms') across the twelve EEC member states. The Cecchini Report had estimated the cost of 'non-Europe' at 4-6% of GDP, providing the economic rationale. The Single Market remains the EU's most significant economic achievement, encompassing over 450 million consumers.

sources.name: European Commission — 30 years of the Single Market

events.title: Maastricht Treaty — European Union Created
events.detail: The Treaty on European Union, signed in Maastricht, Netherlands, transformed the European Communities into the European Union and created the three-pillar structure (Community, CFSP, and Justice & Home Affairs). It introduced EU citizenship, laid out the convergence criteria and timetable for Economic and Monetary Union (the euro), and substantially expanded the European Parliament's co-decision powers. Denmark initially rejected it in a referendum; after opt-outs were agreed, it passed in a second referendum in May 1993.

events.title: Austria, Finland, and Sweden Join the EU
events.detail: Austria, Finland, and Sweden became the 13th, 14th, and 15th members of the European Union, the so-called 'EFTA enlargement.' All three had been members of the European Free Trade Association and had long maintained neutrality during the Cold War. Norway again rejected membership in a referendum. This enlargement brought three wealthy, Nordic/Central European states into the EU, bolstering its institutional capacity.

events.title: Treaty of Amsterdam — Schengen Integrated into EU Law
events.detail: The Treaty of Amsterdam incorporated the Schengen acquis into EU law, expanded the EU's powers in justice and home affairs, strengthened fundamental rights provisions, and made modest reforms to EU institutions. It also introduced the concept of 'enhanced cooperation' allowing subsets of member states to deepen integration. However, it fell short of the institutional reforms many argued were needed before eastern enlargement.

events.title: Euro Launched as Accounting Currency in 11 States
events.detail: The euro was introduced as an electronic accounting currency in eleven EU member states (Austria, Belgium, Finland, France, Germany, Ireland, Italy, Luxembourg, Netherlands, Portugal, Spain) with exchange rates irrevocably fixed. The European Central Bank (ECB), established in June 1998, assumed responsibility for monetary policy. Denmark, Sweden, and the UK exercised opt-outs. Greece joined the Eurozone in 2001.

sources.name: European Central Bank — History of the euro

events.title: Euro Banknotes and Coins Enter Circulation
events.detail: Physical euro banknotes and coins were introduced simultaneously in twelve countries on January 1, 2002 — the largest currency changeover in history. Over 300 million citizens replaced their national currencies (Deutsche Mark, French franc, Italian lira, etc.) within a two-month transition period. The old national currencies ceased to be legal tender by February 28, 2002. The changeover was logistically complex but largely smooth.

sources.name: European Central Bank — Euro cash introduction

events.title: Treaty of Nice — Institutional Reforms for Enlargement
events.detail: The Treaty of Nice reformed EU voting weights, Commission size, and the European Parliament composition to prepare for the large-scale eastern enlargement. It extended qualified majority voting to more policy areas and capped Parliament membership at 732. Ireland rejected the treaty in a first referendum in 2001 before approving it in a second referendum in October 2002. The treaty was widely criticised as insufficient — prompting the subsequent Constitutional Treaty process.

events.title: Historic Eastern Enlargement — Ten New Members Join
events.detail: Ten countries acceded to the EU simultaneously: the Czech Republic, Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Slovakia, Slovenia, Cyprus, and Malta — expanding membership from 15 to 25. This was the largest single enlargement in EU history, adding over 75 million citizens. The accession of eight former communist states was the culmination of post-Cold War integration policy and transformed the EU's centre of gravity eastward.

events.title: Constitutional Treaty Rejected — French and Dutch Referendums
events.detail: French voters rejected the proposed European Constitution by 55% on May 29, 2005. Three days later, Dutch voters rejected it by 61%. The Constitution, which would have replaced all existing EU treaties with a single document, had been ratified by 13 member states. The twin rejections triggered an 18-month 'reflection period' and forced the EU to repackage the reforms into the eventually successful Lisbon Treaty of 2007/2009.

sources.name: BBC — France votes 'No' to EU constitution

events.title: Bulgaria and Romania Join the EU
events.detail: Bulgaria and Romania became the 26th and 27th members of the European Union. Their accession was controversial — both countries were widely regarded as not yet meeting all the Copenhagen Criteria fully, particularly on rule of law and corruption. A special 'Cooperation and Verification Mechanism' (CVM) was established to monitor post-accession judicial reforms. Their entry expanded the EU to over 490 million citizens.

sources.name: European Commission — Bulgaria and Romania Accession

events.title: Treaty of Lisbon Enters into Force
events.detail: The Lisbon Treaty came into force, replacing the failed Constitutional Treaty with a set of amendments to existing treaties. Key changes: creation of a permanent President of the European Council (first: Herman Van Rompuy), creation of a High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy (first: Catherine Ashton), extension of co-decision to most EU legislation, double majority voting in the Council, and an enhanced role for national parliaments. Ireland initially rejected Lisbon in June 2008 before approving it in October 2009.

events.title: Croatia Becomes the 28th EU Member State
events.detail: Croatia acceded to the EU, becoming its 28th member state and the second former Yugoslav republic to join (after Slovenia in 2004). Croatia had completed eight years of accession negotiations after a lengthy process marked by judicial reform benchmarks and border disputes with Slovenia. Croatia was the last EU enlargement for over a decade and joined the Schengen Area fully in January 2023.

events.title: First Greek Bailout — Eurozone Debt Crisis Erupts
events.detail: The EU and IMF agreed to a €110 billion bailout package for Greece after its deficit was revealed to be far larger than reported. This was the first of three Greek bailouts and the opening act of the wider Eurozone sovereign debt crisis that spread to Ireland (November 2010), Portugal (May 2011), Spain (June 2012), and Cyprus (March 2013). The crisis exposed fundamental weaknesses in the Eurozone's fiscal architecture and prompted major institutional reforms including the ESM and banking union.

sources.name: IMF — Greece Article IV and program

events.title: Draghi's 'Whatever It Takes' Speech Stabilises the Euro
events.detail: European Central Bank President Mario Draghi declared at a London conference that the ECB would do 'whatever it takes to preserve the euro,' adding 'and believe me, it will be enough.' This single statement, backed by the subsequent Outright Monetary Transactions (OMT) programme, reversed the trajectory of the Eurozone debt crisis. Italian and Spanish bond yields immediately fell, and no country required a sovereign bailout after this point. The ECB's commitment was later upheld by the European Court of Justice.

events.title: Migration Crisis Peaks — EU Institutional Divisions Exposed
events.detail: Over one million migrants and refugees — primarily from Syria, Afghanistan, and Iraq — crossed into EU territory in 2015, the highest number since World War II. Germany's decision to suspend the Dublin Regulation and announce an open-door policy under Chancellor Merkel's 'Wir schaffen das' ('We can do this') was followed by a temporary closure of borders by several Schengen states. The crisis deeply divided the EU, with Visegrad group countries (Hungary, Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia) refusing mandatory refugee relocation quotas.

events.title: UK Votes to Leave the EU — 52% for Brexit
events.detail: British voters chose to leave the European Union by 51.9% to 48.1% in a referendum held following a renegotiation of UK membership terms by Prime Minister David Cameron. England and Wales voted strongly for Leave; Scotland and Northern Ireland voted for Remain. Cameron resigned the following morning. The result triggered three and a half years of political turmoil, three Prime Ministers, and a parliamentary constitutional crisis before the UK formally departed.

events.title: UK Triggers Article 50 — Brexit Formally Begins
events.detail: Prime Minister Theresa May delivered the Article 50 notification letter to European Council President Donald Tusk, formally beginning the two-year clock for negotiating the UK's withdrawal from the EU. The EU negotiating team was led by Michel Barnier. Three years of tortured negotiations followed, featuring two rejected Withdrawal Agreements, multiple parliamentary defeats, a general election, and three extensions of the Article 50 deadline.

events.title: UK Formally Leaves the European Union
events.detail: The United Kingdom left the EU at 23:00 GMT on January 31, 2020, becoming the first member state ever to leave. The UK entered a transition period until December 31, 2020 during which it remained in the Single Market and Customs Union. The UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA) was agreed on December 24, 2020 and provisionally applied from January 1, 2021, avoiding a no-deal exit but introducing significant trade friction particularly for services and Northern Ireland.

events.title: NextGenerationEU Recovery Fund — €750B Agreed
events.detail: After four days of marathon negotiations, EU leaders agreed on a €750 billion COVID-19 recovery fund, NextGenerationEU, combining €390 billion in grants and €360 billion in loans. For the first time, the EU Commission was authorised to borrow on capital markets at scale to finance shared spending — a 'Hamiltonian moment' hailed as a fundamental shift in EU fiscal integration. The funds were tied to national reform and investment plans reviewed by the Commission.

events.title: EU Vaccine Rollout — AstraZeneca Dispute Erupts
events.detail: The EU's centralised COVID-19 vaccine procurement strategy came under intense criticism after AstraZeneca announced it would deliver far fewer doses than contracted in Q1 2021. The European Commission controversially invoked Article 16 of the Northern Ireland Protocol to control vaccine exports — a move quickly reversed after outcry. The rollout started slower than the UK and US but accelerated significantly by mid-2021, with over 70% of adults vaccinated by September 2021.

events.title: Ukraine and Moldova Granted EU Candidate Status
events.detail: The European Council unanimously agreed to grant EU candidate status to Ukraine and Moldova following Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Ukraine had applied within days of the invasion; the Commission recommended candidate status in June 2022. The decision represented a historic acceleration of enlargement policy. Accession negotiations with Ukraine were formally opened in June 2024.

sources.name: European Council — Ukraine Candidate Status decision

events.title: EU AI Act Passed — World's First Comprehensive AI Regulation
events.detail: The European Parliament adopted the Artificial Intelligence Act by 523 votes to 46, the world's first comprehensive legal framework regulating AI systems. The Act classifies AI applications by risk level (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal) and imposes obligations accordingly. It bans certain uses including real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces and social scoring by governments. Large general-purpose AI models (GPAIs) face transparency and copyright obligations. The regulation enters full force from August 2026.

era: Von der Leyen II & Current Affairs (2024–present)

events.title: European Parliament Elections — Far-Right Surge
events.detail: European Parliament elections were held across 27 EU member states. The centre-right European People's Party (EPP) remained the largest group with 188 seats. The far-right and nationalist groups gained significantly, with ECR and ID parties growing their combined vote share. The results led to weeks of coalition negotiations for the new Parliament's leadership. Ursula von der Leyen was nominated for a second term as Commission President and approved by Parliament in July 2024.

events.title: Draghi Competitiveness Report — €800B Annual Investment Gap Identified
events.detail: Former ECB President and Italian Prime Minister Mario Draghi presented his landmark report 'The future of European competitiveness' to the European Commission. The report identified an annual investment gap of €750-800 billion relative to the US and China, called for a new approach to industrial policy, and recommended reforming state aid rules, completing the Capital Markets Union, and issuing common EU safe assets. It framed EU competitiveness as an existential challenge.

events.title: Accession Negotiations Formally Opened with Ukraine and Moldova
events.detail: The EU formally opened accession negotiations with Ukraine and Moldova in Luxembourg, two years after they were granted candidate status. The opening conference established the framework for negotiations across 35 policy chapters. Ukraine faces a complex reform agenda including rule of law, anti-corruption, and alignment with the EU acquis, while fighting an ongoing war. Moldova's negotiations are proceeding faster given its smaller size and more advanced reform track record.

events.title: EU-US Tariff Tensions — Trump 2.0 Trade War Threat
events.detail: The return of Donald Trump to the US presidency in January 2025 reignited transatlantic trade tensions. The US announced 25% tariffs on European steel and aluminium and threatened broader tariffs across all European goods. The EU prepared retaliatory counter-measures targeting American agricultural products and consumer goods in swing states. The dispute raised concerns about the future of the WTO's dispute settlement system and the long-term resilience of the transatlantic trading relationship.

sources.name: Financial Times — EU-US trade war coverage

events.title: EU-Mercosur Trade Agreement Finalised After 25 Years
events.detail: The European Commission and Mercosur (Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay) finalised the EU-Mercosur Association Agreement after more than 25 years of intermittent negotiations. The deal creates a trade area of over 700 million people. France and several other EU member states strongly opposed it, citing concerns about agricultural competition and deforestation. The agreement faces a complex ratification process requiring approval by the European Parliament and national parliaments.

events.title: EU Defence White Paper — ReArm Europe Initiative Launched
events.detail: The European Commission published its White Paper on European Defence and launched the ReArm Europe plan, proposing a €150 billion defence investment instrument (SAFE — Security Action for Europe) and allowing member states to exclude defence spending from EU deficit rules. The initiative was triggered by Russia's ongoing war in Ukraine and US signals of reduced NATO commitment under the Trump administration. It marked the most significant step toward EU defence integration since the Common Security and Defence Policy was established.

sources.name: European Commission — ReArm Europe White Paper
